question: Universal Containers (UC) operates worldwide, with offices in more than 100 regions in 10 different countries, and has established a very complex Role Hierarchy to control data visibility. In the new fiscal year, UC is planning to reorganize the roles and reassign account owners. Which feature should an architect recommend to avoid problems with this operation?
optionA: Partition data using Divisions
optionB: Skinny table
optionC: Deferred Sharing Recalculation
explanation: Reorganizing roles and reassigning many account owners triggers a massive sharing recalculation that can degrade performance and cause lock errors. Deferred Sharing Recalculation lets the admin suspend automatic group-membership and sharing-rule processing during the bulk change and process it afterward. Divisions only partition data for queries and skinny tables are a read-performance structure, so neither addresses the recalculation load.

question: Universal Containers (UC) delivers training and courses to students and companies. UC is implementing a Salesforce Customer Community for its students granting them a Customer Community license. As part of the community requirements, UC asked to give students access to cases that are related to their account in the UC community. What is the optimum sharing technique to achieve these requirements?
optionA: Use Apex sharing to share cases with the students.
optionB: Create a sharing rule to share cases with the students.
optionC: Create a sharing set to share cases with the students.
explanation: Customer Community licenses have no roles, so role-hierarchy and sharing-rule approaches do not apply and Apex is unnecessary overhead. A Sharing Set is the purpose-built, declarative mechanism that grants community users access to records associated with their own Account or Contact via the lookup, exactly matching cases-by-account. Apex sharing (A) could work but is far more maintenance than the Sharing Set designed for this case.

question: Universal Containers is implementing Sales Cloud. During the final quarter of the financial year, sales managers help each other close deals. They requested a solution in Salesforce to allow them to share opportunities with other sales managers from different teams as needed. They also requested that sharing deals should expire automatically 2 weeks after the new fiscal year starts. Which proposed solution meets the requirements?
optionA: Apex sharing to share opportunities with sales managers
optionB: Scheduled Apex job to remove access
optionC: Sharing rules to share opportunities with sales managers
explanation: Ad-hoc, as-needed sharing among managers from different teams has no fixed role or criteria pattern, so it requires programmatic Apex managed sharing to grant access on demand. A scheduled Apex job then deletes those share rows two weeks into the new fiscal year to satisfy the auto-expiry requirement. Sharing rules (C) are owner- or criteria-based and can neither target arbitrary peers nor expire automatically.

question: At Universal Containers, there's a team of auditors distributed throughout the organization that all need access to high-value opportunities. With a Private sharing model, which option should an architect recommend when designing a solution for this requirement?
optionA: Create a criteria-based sharing rule to grant a public group access to high-value opportunities.
optionB: Add the auditors to the default Opportunity Team.
optionC: Put the auditors at the highest level of the Role Hierarchy.
explanation: With a Private model, a criteria-based sharing rule keyed on the high-value flag grants the auditors (collected in a public group) access regardless of where they sit in the org. Adding them to the Opportunity Team (B) is manual, per-record, and unscalable for a distributed audit team. Placing them atop the Role Hierarchy (C) would expose all opportunities, not just the high-value ones.

question: Universal Containers (UC) uses a custom Lightning component with an Apex class to display shipment information (custom object, Private organization-wide default). UC sales managers are complaining about two important points: Shipment records that belong to their teams can be seen by other users. Shipment amount should be visible only by managers, but sales reps are able to view it. Which feature did the development team miss that is causing the problems?
optionA: Use isShareable keyword in Apex classes to assure record visibility.
optionB: Use isAccessible() method in Apex classes to check field accessibility.
optionC: Use runAs in test class to enforce user permissions and field level permissions.
explanation: The Lightning component's Apex runs in system mode, so Field-Level Security is not automatically enforced and the manager-only amount field leaks to reps. Calling Schema isAccessible() lets the code respect FLS before exposing each field, which is the technique the team missed. isShareable (A) is not a real Apex construct and runAs (C) is a testing method, not a runtime safeguard.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers (UC) is in a legal dispute regarding several orders. UC has found out these records were removed from the system. The VP of Sales has asked to ensure this cannot happen in the future. Which approach should meet this requirement?
optionA: Change the Record Type/Page Layout assignment for Orders to be Read-Only.
optionB: Remove the Delete button from the Order Page Layout.
optionC: Remove Order Delete permission from profiles and permission sets.
explanation: Deleting records is governed by object-level CRUD on profiles and permission sets, so removing the Delete permission is the only reliable way to prevent deletion through any channel, including the API and Apex. Removing the Delete button from a layout (B) merely hides it in the UI and does not stop list-view or API deletes. Record-type or layout changes (A) do not control delete at all.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: A junior account manager owns an account and creates a new opportunity to manage a complex deal. She needs the help of the product specialist and solution engineer. Given the size of this deal, she knows the account is likely to be reassigned to a senior account manager in the near future. What is the optimal way for the junior account manager to share the opportunity, given the private sharing model?
optionA: Manual share on the opportunity
optionB: Manual share on the account
optionC: Opportunity Team
explanation: An Opportunity Team grants the product specialist and solution engineer access that persists with the opportunity even when the account is later reassigned to a senior account manager. A manual share on the opportunity (A) is automatically dropped when ownership changes, breaking the collaboration. Sharing the account (B) does not grant the needed opportunity access.

question: A sales coach at Universal Containers wants to create and share a report folder with other sales coaches. Which two permissions are required to accomplish this?
optionA: Create Report Folders and manage Reports in Public Folders
optionB: Create and customize Reports and Report Folders
optionC: Manage Reports in Public Folders and edit My Reports
explanation: Creating a report folder requires the "Create Report Folders" permission, and sharing or managing what is inside a public report folder requires "Manage Reports in Public Folders"; together they let the coach build and share the folder (option A). Option B covers building reports and customizing them, not the folder-sharing administration this scenario needs. This corrects the source, which selected the report-building option rather than the folder-management pair.

question: Universal Containers uses 75,000 distributors that have close to 1 million total users. Distributors need opportunities assigned to the distributor for delivery. What license recommendation will meet distributor needs?
optionA: Sales Cloud
optionB: Customer Community Plus
optionC: Partner Community
optionD: Customer Community
explanation: Distributors that need opportunities assigned to them for delivery are channel partners performing sales and CRM work, which requires Partner Community because it supports Opportunities and partner roles. Customer Community and Customer Community Plus are oriented to support and self-service, and Customer Community lacks roles and full CRM objects. The one-million-user volume is supported under Partner Community licensing.

question: Universal Containers would like to control access to records and objects according to the following business requirements: Sales users can view all account records but only edit their own records. Sales managers can view all account records but only edit records of their team. Service users can view all account records that are not marked with a RecordType of Prospect. Which organization-wide default configuration should an architect recommend to fulfill these requirements?
optionA: Public Read Write
optionB: Public Read/Transfer
optionC: Private
explanation: Only a Private OWD lets you start from no access and layer the differentiated rules: edit-own for sales users, edit-team for managers via role hierarchy, and record-type-based exclusions for service users. Public Read/Write (A) or Read/Transfer (B) would grant visibility and edit too broadly and could not enforce "edit only your own or your team's" records. A restrictive baseline is the prerequisite for all the layered grants.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a team that analyzes customer orders looking for fraud. This team needs access to Invoice records (custom object, Private organization-wide default). UC has complex rules to control users' access. The architect recommended using Apex managed sharing to meet these requirements. Which recommendation should a developer consider when implementing the changes?
optionA: Use "Without Sharing" keyword to make sure record visibility will be considered.
optionB: Use "With Sharing" keyword to enforce Field-Level Security.
optionC: Use runAs system method to test different users accessing these records.
explanation: Apex managed sharing inserts share rows in system context, so the safeguard against improper access is rigorous testing; runAs() in test methods validates that each user sees only the records intended. The "Without Sharing" keyword (A) would ignore the user's access and worsen the risk, and "With Sharing" does not enforce Field-Level Security, so option B's stated rationale is incorrect.

question: Which functionality does the system method "runAs()" verify when writing test methods?
optionA: Enforcement of a user's record sharing
optionB: Enforcement of a user's Field-Level Security
optionC: Enforcement of a user's permissions
explanation: runAs() changes the running-user context so a test can validate that user's record-level sharing. It does not enforce the user's object permissions or Field-Level Security, which is why B and C are wrong; under runAs, Apex still executes in system mode for CRUD and FLS. Record sharing is the one thing runAs is designed to exercise.

question: A consulting company uses the Salesforce mobile app for its field consultants and uses Case object to track customer specific consulting done by field consultants. The company also has a large number of customer service representatives who takes calls from customers on company issued desktops and uses case object to track customer issues and grievances. The company would like to capture images of customer site captured by field consultants while they are editing the case record during customer site visit. The Director of IT wants to minimize customization and promote reuseability of code artifacts wherever possible. What recommendations should an architect give to the company to implement the image capture requirement, while ensuring customer that the service rep can continue to use same lightning pages they were trained to use?
optionA: Use Lightning Component as an override for "Edit" action on mobile view allowing image capture feature. No Change required for desktop users.
optionB: Use Lightning Component as an override for "Edit" action on lightning experience allowing image capture feature. Detect the form factor of the device and redirect the user to the default not overridden view.
optionC: Create a separate button "Edit in Mobile", which opens a custom lightning component that will allow field consultants to add an image. No change required for desktop users.
explanation: Overriding the Edit action only on the mobile view adds image capture for field consultants while leaving desktop service reps on the standard Lightning pages they were trained on, maximizing reuse and minimizing customization. Option B introduces form-factor detection and redirect complexity, and a separate custom button (C) is extra customization the Director explicitly wanted to avoid.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers requested to leverage Lightning Web Components (LWC) to improve support reps' user experience. LWC will be used as view layer, and Apex classes will have the business logic. Which attention points should the development team consider when implementing this solution?
optionA: Once that Apex runs on system mode, the development team needs to enforce record visibility.
optionB: Create test classes including runAs to test different users accessing the data.
optionC: Use isShareable, isEditable, and isCreatable to enforce field permissions.
explanation: Apex executes in system mode by default, so an LWC-backed controller must explicitly enforce record visibility (for example with the "with sharing" keyword or Security.stripInaccessible) or users will see records they should not. runAs testing (B) is good practice but is not the implementation attention point, and "isSharesble" (C) is not a real method. The system-mode behavior of Apex is the core consideration.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: If a Case requires involvement from one of the Legal team, the Case owner adds one of the corporate lawyers to the Legal SME field on the case. Since cases are private, how can we grant a lawyer access to a specific case when their assistance is needed?
optionA: Create a Criteria-Based Sharing Rule that shares to the Corporate Legal Team Role when the "legal assistance needed?" field is checked.
optionB: Use Apex Managed sharing in a case Trigger that shares the record to the user identified in the Legal SME field.
optionC: Create a Criteria Based Sharing Rule that shares to the Legal Public Group when the "legal assistance needed?" field is checked.
explanation: The lawyer is identified per record in the Legal SME user lookup, so the share target is dynamic and only Apex managed sharing in a Case trigger can share to whichever user populates that field. Criteria-based rules (A and C) can share only to a fixed role or public group, not to an arbitrary user named on each individual case.

question: Universal Containers (UC) delivers training in 500 different regions. The UC operations users team manages course setup, scheduling, and trainer setup. The team members work at a regional level and report to an operations manager. The operations manager requested access to edit ALL scheduled courses owned by the operation users team. How should this be achieved?
optionA: The operations manager will get access to the scheduled courses by creating an ownership-based sharing rule and share the scheduled courses with the operations manager.
optionB: The operations manager will get access to the scheduled courses owned by the operations users team defined in the Role Hierarchy.
optionC: The operations manager will get access to the scheduled courses by creating a public group, and add the operations manager and the operations users team to the public group.
explanation: The operations manager sits above the regional operations users in the Role Hierarchy, and the object grants access up the hierarchy by default, so the manager automatically gets edit access to all scheduled courses the team owns. An ownership-based sharing rule (A) is unnecessary overhead, and a public group (C) does not by itself confer the ownership-based access being requested.

question: Universal Containers uses Person Accounts to represent retail customers and Business Accounts to represent commercial customers. The retail sales team should not have access to commercial customers but should have access to ALL retail customers. With the organization-wide default on Account set to Private, how should the architect meet these requirements?
optionA: Create a criteria-based sharing rule giving the Retail Sales role access to Accounts of type Person Account.
optionB: Create an owner-based sharing rule on AccountContactRelation to grant access to all account contact roles records owned by retail sales reps.
optionC: Update the Retail Sales profile to grant access to Person Account record type.
explanation: With Account set to Private, a criteria-based sharing rule matching the Person Account record type grants the Retail Sales role access to all retail customers while leaving Business (commercial) accounts hidden. Profile record-type access (C) governs creation and layout assignment, not record visibility, and an AccountContactRelation rule (B) does not grant the needed account access.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a partner community for its 200 distributors. UC customer accounts are assigned an individual distributor. The organization-wide default setting for the custom Delivery object is Private. How should an architect advise UC to grant all users at a distributor access to delivery records for all customers assigned to a particular distributor?
optionA: Create a criteria based sharing rule that shares delivery records matching a distributor to the Distributor role in the Role Hierarchy.
optionB: Create a criteria-based sharing rule that shares delivery records matching the Distributor to users of a Public Group created for the distributor.
optionC: Create a Sharing Set for the Distributor profile to grant access to the Delivery object.
explanation: Partner Community users belong to a profile, and a Sharing Set grants all users under a distributor access to Delivery records tied to that distributor's account through the lookup, which is the scalable, declarative answer for community users. Criteria- or role-based sharing rules (A and B) are designed for internal role-based access, not high-volume community accounts.

question: Universal Containers' organization wide-defaults model is Private for the Account object. A sales rep owns two opportunities in one Account. The Sales Rep profile has Create/Edit access to opportunity records. Which level of access will the sales rep have to the related Account record?
optionA: Read access
optionB: Read/Create/Edit access
optionC: No access
explanation: With the Account OWD Private, owning a child opportunity grants implicit Read-only access to the parent account so the related record is viewable. Implicit parent sharing never grants edit or create on the parent, so Read/Create/Edit (B) and No access (C) are incorrect.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has created a custom Invoice object. Standard sales users at UC can see the records in search layout, but when they click to view the detail, only record name, created date, and last modified date are shown. When the system admin accesses it, he or she sees the full record detail with many more data fields. What is the likely cause of this issue?
optionA: A role-based sharing rule is missing and should be added for the sales user's role to grant access to the fields.
optionB: The Sales Users profile does not have access to the remaining fields.
optionC: The page layout assigned to Sales User profile has only Read-Only access to the fields.
explanation: The user can open the record but sees only a few system fields while the admin sees everything, which is the signature of missing Field-Level Security on the Sales Users profile. A sharing rule (A) controls record access, not field visibility, and a read-only page layout (C) would still display the fields rather than hide them. FLS is what determines which fields a profile can read.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers (UC) wants to reduce the amount of redundant leads entered into the system. UC also only edited/reassigned by the lead owner. What organization-wide default (OWD) approach should be recommended to help UC implement these requirements?
optionA: Implement a Public Read Only OWD on Lead.
optionB: Implement a Public Read Only/Transfer OWD on Lead
optionC: Implement a private OWD on Lead.
optionD: Implement a Public Read/Write OWD on Lead.
explanation: To reduce duplicate leads, users must be able to see existing leads before creating new ones, so Public Read Only exposes all leads while reserving edit and reassignment to the owner. A Private model (C, the source answer) would hide existing leads and increase duplication, and Read/Write or Read/Transfer would over-grant edit and transfer rights. This corrects the source and is consistent with Q80.

question: Universal Containers would like to store an encryption key within Salesforce for use in Apex Code, but does not want users to be able to see this confidential key. Users require the view setup permission. How can Universal Containers securely store the confidential key?
optionA: Create a protected custom metadata type that stores the encryption key. Package the protected custom metadata type with its associated records in a managed package. Install the package in the production org.
optionB: Create a protected custom metadata type that stores the encryption key. Package the protected custom metadata type with its associated records in an unlocked package. Install the package in the production org.
optionC: Create a custom metadata type that stores the encryption key. Make sure that only the admin profile has access to the custom metadata type so that end users cannot access the encryption key.
explanation: A protected custom metadata type packaged in a managed package hides its records even from users who hold View Setup, so the encryption key stays invisible while remaining usable in Apex. Plain custom metadata in the subscriber org (C) is viewable through setup, and an unlocked package (B) does not protect the records the same way a managed package does.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers (UC) is a fast-growing company that sells containers globally. It has thousands of dealerships throughout the world where local dealers service containers. UC implemented a new Partner Community for its dealers. Each dealership has a dealer manager who manages the service agents. The community sharing model is private. What is the optimal option to give dealer managers visibility to customer cases only raised by their service agents?
optionA: Use the Role Hierarchy so that the cases raised by service agents will be visible to their relevant dealer managers.
optionB: Create a sharing rule to share cases created by service agents with their dealer manager.
optionC: Create a sharing group to share cases created by service agents with their dealer manager.
explanation: In a private community model, dealer managers sit above their service agents in the partner Role Hierarchy, so cases owned by the agents roll up to their relevant manager automatically with no extra configuration. A sharing rule (B) is unnecessary, and a "sharing group" (C) is not the correct construct for this hierarchy-based visibility.

question: The finance team at Universal Containers usually does not have access to account and contact records. A finance analyst was temporarily given opportunity access for a big deal to help with tax calculation. However, she can now also access account and contact records. What is causing this issue?
optionA: Account records can be accessed due to implicit sharing from Opportunity.
optionB: Account records can be accessed due to Role Hierarchy.
optionC: Contact records can be accessed due to implicit sharing from Opportunity.
explanation: Granting access to an opportunity gives implicit Read access to its parent account and related contacts, which is why the finance analyst can suddenly see the account; this is by-design implicit parent sharing from the opportunity. The cause is the opportunity access, not the Role Hierarchy (B), and the question concerns the account specifically.

question: Universal Containers has implemented Customer Community with Customer Community Plus licenses for its distributors. Some distributors requested granting specific community users (agents) to view cases submitted by other agents of the same distributor. Which feature only supports these requirements?
optionA: Permission set to grant community admin permission
optionB: Delegate external user
optionC: Partner super user
explanation: Customer Community Plus has roles, and the Partner/Customer Super User permission lets a user see records owned by other users of the same account at their role and below, which is exactly "view cases of other agents of the same distributor." A community-admin permission set (A) or delegated external user administration (B) manage users, not record visibility.

question: Who can view a PDF that is uploaded to the Files Home private library by a user?
optionA: The user and users above them in the Role Hierarchy
optionB: The user and users with View All Data permission
optionC: Only the user
explanation: Files uploaded to a user's private library in Files Home are visible only to that user until explicitly shared, so neither the Role Hierarchy nor View All Data grants access. That is why options A and B are incorrect; private-library content is genuinely private by default.

question: Which method should be used to grant access to an unrelated group of users?
optionA: Sharing Sets
optionB: Public Groups
optionC: Role Hierarchy
explanation: Public Groups are the mechanism for grouping an arbitrary, unrelated set of users across roles so they can be targeted by sharing. Sharing Sets apply to community users tied to an account or contact, and the Role Hierarchy only models reporting lines, so neither grants access to an unrelated group.

question: Besides their own team accounts, sales managers at Universal Containers (UC) need Read access to all accounts of the same segment in other countries. Role Hierarchy was implemented accordingly (based on countries), but a sales manager in the U.S. is complaining that he cannot view account records of the same segment in Canada. What should UC do to grant access properly?
optionA: Create an owner-based sharing rule to grant access to account records, that have the same segment, to all sales manager roles.
optionB: Create a public group and include all accounts of the same segment, and then grant access with a permission set.
optionC: Change the Role Hierarchy and put all the sales managers in the U.S. and Canada in the same role.
explanation: A sharing rule that shares same-segment accounts to every sales-manager role gives the U.S. manager visibility into Canadian same-segment accounts that the country-based Role Hierarchy cannot provide laterally. Merging the roles (C) would break the country hierarchy, and a permission set (B) cannot grant record access based on segment. Note the option says "owner-based," though sharing on a segment value is technically criteria-based, so the wording is imprecise.

question: Dreamforce presenters need to be able to edit their presentation details (summary, presenter biographies, etc) on a private custom object in Salesforce (Presentation). All presenters for a presentation are captured on a Presenters junction object between Presenter and User. How can this be accomplished?
optionA: Give Edit rights to the Presentation record via a Permission set that is given to the Presenters for a record.
optionB: Trigger on Presenter junction object that adds the user to the Sales Team for the Presentation record.
optionC: Trigger on Presenter junction object that uses Apex Managed sharing to add or remove access to the related Presentation record.
explanation: Presenters are linked through a Presenters junction and need edit access on the related private Presentation, so a trigger that uses Apex managed sharing to add or remove the user's access as junction records change is the precise solution. A permission set (A) grants object-level access rather than record-level, and adding to a Sales Team (B) does not apply to this custom object.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has affiliates who sell containers in countries where UC does not have a local office. UC uses a Partner Community to manage the sales cycle in those countries. One of its affiliates has grown substantially and restructured its internal sales team with the following structure: Sales VP -> Director of Sales -> Sales Manager -> Sales Reps. Sales Reps should not see each others' opportunities, and visibility of deals should open up within a Sales Rep's hierarchy. What is the main problem an architect should address in this situation?
optionA: The Channel Manager role cannot be shared with Partner Community.
optionB: Partner User roles are limited to three Ievels.
optionC: Partner Community does not support Role Hierarchy.
explanation: Partner Community provides only three partner role levels, so a four-tier reseller hierarchy of VP, Director, Manager, and Rep cannot be modeled directly, which is the core problem an architect must address. Partner Community does support a role hierarchy (so C is false); the limitation is the depth, not the existence, of partner roles.

question: Universal Containers uses the standard Case object to track various support requests sent to servicing agents. Currently, the Case object has more than 400 fields. Eight different types of case processes have been established, each with a unique combination of statuses. The Director of Customer Service wants to ensure that servicing agents see only the required information specific to the case type they are addressing. Which option is recommended to implement this requirement?
optionA: Use Dynamic Forms with different field sections representing the relevant fields per case type, and control visibility of sections by Case Record Type value.
optionB: Use different Page Layouts per case type, each representing the relevant fields per case type.
optionC: Use a custom LWC to override the View action of the Case object, and a Custom Metadata Type to define the relevant fields per case type.
explanation: Dynamic Forms lets you place fields in sections and show or hide them by Case Record Type on a single Lightning page, meeting the requirement to show only the relevant fields per case type with minimal layouts. Multiple page layouts (B) is the classic alternative and is defensible, but it is heavier to maintain across eight processes and 400 fields. A custom LWC override (C) adds unnecessary code.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: A custom Invoice object has been created with a master-detail relationship to Account. The accounts receivable (AR) team needs access to invoice records. AR users neither own nor have access to account records. The Account organization-wide default is set to Private. The AR team is unable to find invoices in list views, reports, and Global Search. The architect has been asked to help troubleshoot. What is preventing AR team members from seeing invoices?
optionA: The AR profile does not have Read permission to the Invoice object.
optionB: A sharing rule is missing to share invoices with the AR team.
optionC: A sharing rule is missing to share accounts with the AR team.
explanation: Because Invoice is master-detail to Account, invoice access is Controlled by Parent, so the AR team cannot see invoices because they cannot see the parent accounts; sharing the Accounts with AR restores visibility everywhere, including list views, reports, and Global Search. The team already has object Read (so A is wrong), and you cannot directly share a detail record (so B is wrong).

question: Universal Containers has expanded to sell virtual containers for data storage. Virtual container work orders are provisioned immediately by the system and therefore cannot be changed by a sales rep. What is an optimal approach to implement these requirements?
optionA: Change the Record Type/Page Layout assignment for Work Orders to Read Only.
optionB: Implement a sharing rule that changes access for all Work Orders to Read.
optionC: Remove the Work Order Edit permission from the Sales Representative profile.
explanation: Provisioned virtual work orders must be non-editable by reps, and removing the Edit object permission on the Sales Representative profile is the clean, system-wide enforcement. Record-type or layout read-only settings (A) do not block edits made through the API, and a sharing rule (B) controls record access rather than edit capability at the object level.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Users at Universal Containers are complaining that a field has disappeared from the Account page after last weekend's deployment. The page layout did not change with this deployment. How should the admin troubleshoot this issue?
optionA: View Field Accessibility in the Object Manager.
optionB: Log in as a user and check several accounts to isolate the problem records.
optionC: Run a Who Sees What report, filtering on Account.
explanation: A field disappearing without a layout change points to a Field-Level Security or accessibility change, and the Field Accessibility viewer in Object Manager shows per profile and record type whether a field is hidden or read-only, pinpointing the cause quickly. Logging in as a user (B) or running a Who-Sees-What report (C) is slower and less targeted for diagnosing a single field.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: An architect has a requirement to create a criteria-based sharing rule based on the customer Social Security Number. However, when setting up the rule in Contact Sharing, the field is not shown on the list of available fields. What is causing this issue?
optionA: The architect does not have permission to Compliance fields.
optionB: The field has been configured for encryption.
optionC: The architect's profile does not have Field-Level Security for this field.
explanation: Classic-encrypted fields cannot be used in criteria-based sharing rule filters, so the encrypted Social Security Number field is absent from the available-fields list, which is the platform restriction at play. Lacking FLS (C) would hide the field on layouts but is not why it is specifically unavailable for sharing-rule criteria.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Which option can be selected to share data when creating a sharing rule?
optionA: Roles
optionB: users
optionC: Profiles
explanation: When creating a sharing rule, the "share with" target can be public groups, roles, or roles and subordinates, so among these options Roles is the valid choice. You cannot share directly to individual users or to profiles in a sharing rule, which makes B and C incorrect.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is a non-profit organization with more than 20,000,000 members (donors). UC decided to assign those accounts to donations reps based on their regions. Donations reps ended up owning more than 50,000 donors each. The donation reps started to see significant degradation of the system performance. What is the reason for this problem?
optionA: The donations reps' access to the assigned accounts is wrong.
optionB: Salesforce sharing recalculation kicked off.
optionC: There is an Account ownership data skew problem.
explanation: Tens of thousands of accounts owned by individual donation reps creates account ownership data skew, which causes sharing and recalculation contention and the observed performance degradation. The access design itself is not wrong (A), and recalculation (B) is a symptom rather than the root cause; the skew is the underlying problem.

question: A sales rep at Universal Containers was added to an opportunity team with Read/Write permissions. Which action is she allowed to perform in the opportunity?
optionA: Replace opportunity owner.
optionB: Update opportunity stage.
optionC: Add/remove members in the opportunity team.
explanation: An Opportunity Team member with Read/Write can edit the record's fields, such as updating the stage. They cannot replace the owner or add and remove team members, because those are owner-only actions, which is why A and C are not allowed.

question: A sales rep (John) at Universal Containers (UC) requested to update information in an account record where he has READ only access. John requested the EDIT access permission from the owner of the record (Paul). Paul manually shared the record with John. Assuming the OWD of the Account object is Public Read-Only, what is the impact in the system?
optionA: New AccountShare record is created. Row Cause is "Manual" and Access Level is "Read/Write".
optionB: Existing AccountShare record is updated. Row Cause is "Owner" and User/Group is "John".
optionC: New AccountShare record is created. Row Cause is "Owner" and Access Level is "Full".
optionD: Existing AccountShare record is updated. Row Cause is "Manual" and Access Level is "Read/Write".
explanation: Manually granting Read/Write on a Public Read-Only account creates a new AccountShare row with row cause "Manual" and access level Read/Write. The baseline Read-only access comes from the OWD rather than from an "Owner" share row that gets updated, so the descriptions in B, C, and D mischaracterize the resulting share record.

question: Sales Operations at Universal Containers (UC) has created Public Report and Dashboard folders for sales managers that report to the VP of sales. Sales Operations currently spends a few hours each month updating users that should have access to edit items in these folders. In which two ways can UC grant access to sales managers to automate access to these Reports and Dashboards folders? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Share the folders lowest roles in the role hierarchy, Superiors will get access automatically.
optionB: Share the folders with the "VP of Sales" Role and Subordinates.
optionC: Share the folders with the "Sales Managers" Queue.
optionD: Share the folders with a "Sales Managers" Public Group.
explanation: Report and dashboard folders can be shared with a role and its subordinates or with a public group, both of which automatically include the right managers and eliminate the monthly manual upkeep. Sharing to the lowest roles expecting superiors to inherit (A) is false because folder access does not roll up the hierarchy, and folders cannot be shared with queues (C).

question: Sales reps at Universal Containers (UC) complain about the manual activities they need to perform in order to grant access to supporting internal users (legal, engineering, finance, etc.) for customer records when they need help. In general, the sales reps involved in the deals will not change. How should an architect help UC improve sales reps' productivity?
optionA: Create a permission set with View All Data and assign to supporting users.
optionB: Create a criteria based sharing rule to grant access to other users.
optionC: Leverage default Account team.
explanation: Because the same supporting users repeatedly help on the rep's deals, a default Account Team auto-adds them to new accounts, removing the manual sharing while scoping access appropriately. View All Data (A) massively over-grants, and a criteria-based rule (B) does not fit the pattern of specific recurring helpers per rep.

question: An architect from a previous project implemented Platform Shield Encryption for a company. However, based on a recent audit, the company's Privacy Team identified three additional fields in their Account Records (Billing Street, Billing City and Phone) that needs to be secure and protected. How should an architect proceed with this new policy change?
optionA: Use Encryption Policy and contact Salesforce to update the existing records so that their field values are encrypted.
optionB: Use Encryption Policy and wait for an email from Salesforce indicating the field values are encrypted.
optionC: Use Classic Encryption to ensure all fields are protected and contact Salesforce for help with encryption verification.
explanation: Turning on Shield Platform Encryption for additional fields encrypts new and changed data, but existing stored values must be encrypted through a background process you request from Salesforce. Classic Encryption (C) is a separate, weaker feature and is not the path for Shield-protected standard fields. Note that self-service background encryption has since been introduced, so this platform detail may have changed.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: (Variant) Which method should be used to grant an unrelated group of users access to a set of records?
optionA: Role Hierarchy
optionB: Sharing Sets
optionC: Public Groups
explanation: (Variant of Q26.) An unrelated set of users is grouped with a Public Group, which a sharing rule then targets to grant record access. Sharing Sets apply to community users via account or contact, and the Role Hierarchy models reporting lines, so neither fits arbitrary users. This corrects the source, which selected Sharing Sets.

question: What advanced tool can Salesforce enable for Large-scale role hierarchy realignments?
optionA: Set external organization-wide default to public read only
optionB: Skinny Table Indexing
optionC: Granular locking
optionD: Partitioning by Divisions
explanation: For large-scale role-hierarchy realignments, Granular Locking is the advanced feature Salesforce can enable; it locks only the affected group-membership subsets instead of the whole table, allowing parallel processing and avoiding lock errors. External OWD, skinny-table indexing, and Divisions do not address recalculation locking.

question: Sales operations at Universal Containers (UC) wants to create list views to filter opportunities for certain geographies. How should UC hide list views that are not relevant to an individual user since there will be more than 50 list views?
optionA: Share the list views with the appropriate public group.
optionB: Share the list views with the appropriate queue.
optionC: Share the list views with the appropriate individual users.
explanation: List views are made visible to a chosen set of users by sharing them with the appropriate public group, so each geography's reps see only the views relevant to them. List views can be shared with public groups or roles but not with queues (B), and per-user sharing (C) does not scale across more than fifty views.

question: To grant Universal Containers sales managers access to shipment records properly, it was necessary to leverage Apex managed sharing. The IT team is worried about improper access to records. What should an architect recommend to mitigate this risk?
optionA: Use isShareable keyword in Apex classes to assure record visibility will be followed.
optionB: Use isAccessible keyword in Apex classes to assure record visibility will be followed.
optionC: Use runAs system method in test classes to test using different users and profiles.
explanation: Apex managed sharing runs in system context, so the safeguard against improper access is testing with runAs() across different users and profiles to confirm only the intended visibility. The "isSharesble" and "isAccesible keyword" options (A and B) are not valid record-visibility constructs in Apex.

question: Universal Containers has selected a small and diverse group of users to review Inactive accounts. Given the Private sharing model, a public group was created and made available to this group of users. A sharing rule was created to make inactive Accounts visible to the public group. However, some of these users are reporting they don't see any of the Accounts that were shared with the public group. what is the underlying Issue for these users?
optionA: The users are in profiles that have no access to the Account object.
optionB: The page layout assigned to these users is different than the Account owner.
optionC: The users have a permission set that only allow Accounts in "Active' status.
optionD: The Accounts are owned by users higher in the role hierarchy.
explanation: Sharing rules extend record access only on top of object-level CRUD, so users whose profiles lack Read on the Account object see nothing regardless of the sharing rule. A differing page layout (B) or a status-limited permission set (C) would not explain a complete absence of the shared accounts. Object access is the prerequisite the affected users are missing.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: A user posts a file to the Chatter feed for a record of an object that has a Private organization-wide default. Which statement accurately describes who can view the file by default?
optionA: Only the user who posted the file
optionB: The user who posted the file and users with access to the record
optionC: The user who posted the file and users with a shared chatter post link to the file
explanation: A file posted to a record's Chatter feed inherits that record's sharing, so it is visible to the poster and to anyone who has access to the private record. It is not limited to only the poster (A) and does not depend on a shared post link (C).

question: Which community function is impacted by having the Site User Visibility turned off in Sharing Settings?
optionA: Searching for other external users.
optionB: Updating their user profile.
optionC: Searching for internal users.
explanation: Disabling Site (community) User Visibility stops community users from finding other external users in search; it does not affect a user editing their own profile or searching for internal users. That is why B and C are not the impacted function.

question: The sales managers at Universal Containers requested their teams to define each user's role on their accounts in order to provide an easy way to establish accountability and collaboration. Sales managers also requested that sales associates should only get the following permissions: 1. Read access to the accounts. 2. Read access to cases related to the accounts. 3. No access to deals related to the accounts. The sales associates may be granted access to opportunities when needed. Assuming the overall sharing model of the organization is Private and no sharing rules are configured on the Account object, how should an architect achieve these requirements?
optionA: Use Account teams to define access to accounts as well as opportunities and cases related to accounts.
optionB: Use Account teams and Case teams. No configuration required for the Opportunity object.
optionC: Use Account teams and sharing rules to share cases with sales associates. No change required to the Opportunity object.
explanation: A single Account Team can grant the defined per-user access, namely Read on accounts and related cases and no opportunity access unless explicitly granted, covering all three requirements through one mechanism. Splitting into Case teams and sharing rules (B and C) is unnecessary because Account Team access settings already control related case and opportunity access.

question: Customer complaints for bad interactions with a customer support agent are logged as Cases and assigned to a human resources representative. The agent of the complaint should not see the case, but their manager should. How is this accomplished?
optionA: Trigger on Case to lookup and share to the manager of an Assigned Agent custom field (the subject of the complaint) using Apex Managed Sharing.
optionB: Criteria based Sharing Rule on Case that shares to the Role Manager and above when a custom field Assigned Agent (subject of the complaint) Is not blank,
optionC: Case is owned by the subject of the complaint, so their manager in the role hierarchy can access the record. CRED permission are removed on Case so the agent cannot read the case record.
explanation: The complaint must be hidden from the agent but visible to their manager, identified through a custom Assigned Agent field, so a trigger that looks up that agent's manager and shares via Apex managed sharing is the precise solution. A criteria-based rule to "Role Manager and above" (B) over-shares up the chain, and owning the case by the subject (C) would let the agent see it.

question: Universal Containers has created a Reimbursement Custom Object to capture requests for reimbursement of expenses related to office supplies. The Reimbursement requests are sometimes sensitive and the Team Leads should not have access to the Reimbursement records submitted by the Users in the Call Center Agent role. The Director of Support will require access to all Reimbursement records. The role hierarchy is set as follows: Call Center Agent role reports to Team Lead role, which Reports to the Director of Support. Which steps would the Architect take to ensure proper sharing in this Role Hierarchy structure assuming Private Sharing Setting for the Reimbursement Object?
optionA: Leave the Reimbursement Object in "Deployed" Status and set the Director of Supports Profile to "View All" in the object permissions.
optionB: Use an Approval Process to change the owner of the Reimbursement record upon submission to the Director of Support
optionC: Disable Grant Access Using Hierarchies for the Object and create a Sharing Rule to enable sharing to the Director of Support
optionD: The sharing scenario described will occur without any special configuration choices being made by the Architect
explanation: Because the Director sits above the Team Leads, the requirement to hide reimbursements from Team Leads while still giving the Director access means you must disable "Grant Access Using Hierarchies" on the object, then add a sharing rule granting the Director access. Setting View All on the Director's profile (A) does not stop the Team Lead from inheriting subordinate records, and the default behavior (D) would expose the records to Team Leads.

question: Universal Containers has a global 24x7 Salesforce.com implementation that supports Sales, Services, Order Management, and various other parts of their business. They have a nested territory hierarchy, 10,000 sales users, and 20,000 support agents. Territory changes happen daily. The demand for new applications and changes to the platform is high and they follow an agile development methodology and deliver new releases every two weeks on the platform. What Salesforce.com feature would help the system recover from a maintenance restart on Salesforce.com servers?
optionA: Enable Granular Locking on the system.
optionB: Enable Deferred Sharing Rule recalculation.
optionC: Enable Parallel Sharing Rule recalculation.
optionD: Enable Filter -Based Opportunity Territory Assignment.
explanation: With daily territory changes and high transaction volume, Deferred Sharing recalculation lets admins suspend and batch sharing recalculation, helping the org catch up after a maintenance restart without a recalculation storm. Granular and Parallel recalculation (A and C) improve throughput, but the recover-after-restart framing points to deferring recalculation. The "recover from maintenance restart" wording is somewhat ambiguous.

question: Universal Containers has a strict security model enforced through object, field, and row-based security mechanisms. The Architect would like to ensure the security model is being thoroughly tested using Apex automated tests. Which three considerations should be made when using the runAs() method to design Apex until tests?
optionA: runAs() does not enforce user permissions or field-level permissions in test classes
optionB: runAs() can be used inside of test classes to validate record-level security
optionC: runAs() can be used inside of test classes to validate field-level permissions
optionD: runAs() can be used outside of test classes to bypass record-level security
optionE: runAs () counts towards total DML statements issued within transaction
explanation: runAs() switches the running user only for record-level sharing validation, so it does not enforce object or field permissions (A is a true consideration), it can validate record-level security (B), and each runAs counts as a DML statement (E). It cannot be used outside test classes (D is false) and does not enforce Field-Level Security (C is false).

question: An External Object is created to show Invoices from an external accounting system. When viewing the External Object, a user should only access invoice records the user is authorized to see. What two actions are required to achieve the above requirement? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Setup External Object to use OAuth to connect to the Accounting system.
optionB: Create an owner based sharing rule to grant visibility to the Invoice object.
optionC: Restrict access to data in the accounting system.
optionD: Grant access to the External Object to only the Account Manager profile.
explanation: External Object rows live in the external system, so per-user authorization is enforced there: connect via OAuth so each user authenticates as themselves (A), and restrict the data in the accounting system itself (C). Salesforce sharing rules (B) and profile grants (D) do not filter external-object rows on a per-record basis.

question: Which three capabilities are available with Enterprise Territory Management? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Create a public group with Territory
optionB: Share a report or dashboard folder with a Territory
optionC: Metadata API Support
optionD: Integration with Collaborative Forecasting
explanation: Enterprise Territory Management supports the Metadata API (C), integrates with Collaborative Forecasting (D), and assigns territories to opportunities (E). You cannot create a public group "with Territory" (A) or share a report or dashboard folder with a territory (B), so those are not ETM capabilities.

question: Universal Containers has set Partners users who will see records owned by partner users in roles below them in the hierarchy of which roles?
explanation: The partner portal and community role hierarchy provides three role levels per account, Executive, Manager, and User, and a partner user can see records owned by partner users below them among those roles. These three named levels are the standard partner role tiers.

question: (Variant) Susan posts a file to the Chatter feed for a record of an object which OWD is private. Which two statements accurately describe who can view the file by default? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Susan and users with the View All Data permission.
optionB: Susan and users with access to the record.
optionC: Susan and users with a shared chatter post link to the file.
optionD: Susan only.
explanation: (Variant of Q47.) A file posted to a private record's Chatter feed is visible to the poster, to users with access to the record, and to users with View All Data, so both "View All Data" and "access to the record" are correct. It is not limited to Susan alone or to a shared post link.

question: Universal Containers created a custom object called Defect and would like to enable the Sharing button on the Defect page layout to share the records manually to other users in the organization. In which scenario will the Sharing button appear?
optionA: The Sharing model is either Private or Public Read only.
optionB: The Sharing button always appears.
optionC: The Sharing model is Public Read/Write.
optionD: The Sharing model is Public Read/Write/Transfer.
explanation: The Sharing button appears only when the object's OWD is more restrictive than Public Read/Write, that is Private or Public Read Only, because manual sharing makes sense only when there is access to grant. Under Public Read/Write everyone already has full access, so the button is not shown, which makes B, C, and D incorrect.

question: A custom ServiceFeedback object is used to collect partner feedback. ServiceFeedback records should be available to all internal employees. The OWD is set to Private for external users so partners cannot see feedback from other partner users. How can the Architect give access to all internal employees?
optionA: Create a trigger on ServiceFeedback to change ownership to an internal employee.
optionB: Ensure all the internal users are above the partners in the role hierarchy.
optionC: Create an Owner based sharing rule for all ServiceFeedback records owned by Partners.
optionD: Set OWD, for Internal Users to Public Read Only.
explanation: With separate internal and external OWD, you can keep external (partner) access Private so partners cannot see each other's feedback while setting the internal OWD to Public Read Only so all employees can read every ServiceFeedback record. Ownership triggers or sharing rules (A and C), and stacking all internal users in the hierarchy (B), are unnecessary when split OWD solves it directly.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has implemented Customer Community with customer community plus license for its distributors. Retail distributors have part-time agents who work for multiple distributors. Those agents should have access to other distributor accounts as well. What can be configured to grant agents access to different distributor accounts?
optionA: Use Apex sharing to share distributor accounts with agents using the Account__ Share table.
optionB: Use share group to share distributor accounts with agents who have account contact relationship.
optionC: Create sharing rules to share distributor accounts with agents.
optionD: Create sharing set to share distributor accounts with agents using account contact relationship.
explanation: Part-time agents work across multiple unrelated distributor accounts, so the relationship is not captured by a single account-contact link; Apex managed sharing writing to the Account share table can grant each agent the specific extra accounts. A sharing set keyed to the account-contact relationship (D) only covers the agent's own account, so it is arguably more declarative but does not satisfy the multi-account requirement here.

question: Universal Containers has developed an AppExchange managed package for their distribution partners, which required a private key to be generated for each partner and used by the code. Universal Containers support representatives must be able to access the private key value to debug connection issues, but it must not be possible for the partner to access the value. How can the Architect best support this requirement?
optionA: Store the value in a text field on a protected custom setting in the package.
optionB: Store the value in a static variable in a class included in the managed package.
optionC: Store the value in the text field on a list custom setting in the managed package.
optionD: Store the value in an encrypted field on a custom object in the package.
explanation: Support reps must read the key value to debug while partners must not, so an encrypted field on a packaged custom object lets reps with the View Encrypted Data permission see it while partners cannot, satisfying both constraints. A protected custom setting (A) hides the value from everyone including support, and a static variable (B) is not retrievable as stored data.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers has created a custom object to store highly confidential client relationship data with Private sharing settings. Which two options would an Architect choose to ensure access only to the record owner and the administrator? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Disable "Grant Access Using Hierarchies."
optionB: Disable the "Create" permission on all other profiles.
optionC: Disable the "Read" permission on all other profiles.
optionD: Disable the "View All" permission on all other profiles.
explanation: To restrict a Private custom object to only the owner and the administrator, disable "Grant Access Using Hierarchies" so managers do not inherit access (A), and remove "View All" from other profiles so no one else can read all records (D). Disabling Create or Read on profiles (B and C) would also remove the owner's own ability to work with their records.

question: Universal Containers has a customer that meets criteria for two Enterprise territory Management territories (Portugal and Southern Europe). What is necessary to assign opportunities to a territory for this account?
optionA: Create a criteria-based sharing rule on the Opportunity to assign It to a territory.
optionB: Create a Process Builder Process that updates the Territory field on the Opportunity
optionC: The territory with the highest Territory Type Priority is automatically assigned to the Opportunity
optionD: Create an Apex class that implement. Filter-Based Opportunity Territory Assignment
explanation: When an account qualifies for multiple ETM territories, opportunities are not automatically assigned to a single territory; you must implement the Apex class for Filter-Based Opportunity Territory Assignment to decide. The claim that the highest Territory Type Priority auto-assigns (C, the source answer) is false, because priority only feeds the Apex logic rather than assigning on its own.

question: (Variant) ...From customers on company Issued desktops and uses case object to track customer issues and grievances. The company would like to capture images of customer site captured by field consultants while they are editing the case record during customer site visit. The Director of IT wants to minimize customization and promote reusability of code artifacts wherever possible, what recommendations should an architect give to the company to implement the image capture requirement, while ensuring customer that the service rep can continue to use same lightning pages they were trained to use?
optionA: Use Lightning Component as an override for "Edit" action on Lightning Experience allowing image capture feature. Detect the form factor of the device and redirect the user to the default not overridden view.
optionB: Create a separate button "Edit in Mobile", which opens a custom Lightning component that will allow field consultants to add an image. No change required for desktop users.
optionC: Use Lightning Component as an override for "Edit'' action on mobile view allowing image capture feature. No Change required for desktop users.
explanation: (Variant of Q12.) Overriding the Edit action only on the mobile view delivers image capture to field consultants while desktop service reps keep their standard Lightning pages, minimizing customization and maximizing reuse. Form-factor redirect (A) and a separate custom button (B) add unnecessary complexity.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers has a Performance Feedback custom object (Private organization-wide default) used by customers to report any Issues with delivery drivers. Feedback should not be visible to the driver, but any feedback records should be accessible to people above them in the Role Hierarchy, even when the driver changes managers. Assuming managers have Read access to the Feedback object, which three steps are necessary to solve these requirements?
optionA: Remove Read permission on the Driver profile, have feedback ownership transferred to the driver's manager when feedback Is submitted, and use the Role Hierarchy to give access to a driver's manager.
optionB: Remove Read permission on the Driver profile, have feedback ownership transferred to the driver when feedback is submitted, and use the Role Hierarchy to give access to a driver's manager.
optionC: Remove Read permission on the Driver profile, have feedback ownership transferred to the driver when feedback is submitted, and create an ownership-based sharing rule,
explanation: Feedback must be hidden from the driver yet visible to managers even as managers change, so keep the driver as owner (so the dynamic Role Hierarchy always routes access to whoever is currently above them) and remove Read on the Driver profile so the owner still cannot see it. An ownership-based sharing rule (C, the source answer) is static and breaks when the manager changes, and transferring ownership to the manager (A) loses the driver linkage.

question: What should a Salesforce architect recommend to make sure that users that gained access to a custom of sharing do not lose access to it when its owner is changed?
optionA: Create a specific Sharing Reason for the custom object.
optionB: Use ''runAS'' system method in Apex classes to enforce record visibility.
optionC: Create a new record in the Share object with RowCause "Manual".
optionD: Use ''With Sharing'' keyword to make sure record visibility will be considered
explanation: Apex managed sharing rows tagged with a custom Apex Sharing Reason are preserved when record ownership changes, whereas "Manual" row-cause shares (C) are deleted on owner change. The runAs and "With Sharing" options (B and D) are unrelated to whether a share row persists.

question: Universal Containers has a custom object, Employee Review, with an Organization-Wide Default security setting of Private. A user lookup on the Employee Review object is populated when a reviewer is assigned to perform a review. How can this user be granted edit access to the record if they are not the owner?
optionA: The user will be granted access to the record automatically when the user lookup is populated.
optionB: Create a workflow rule to share the Employee Review record with the user in the lookup field.
optionC: Create an Apex trigger to insert an Employee Review Share record with an access level of Edit.
optionD: Create a criteria-based sharing rule to share the record with the user in the lookup field.
explanation: A user named in a lookup needs edit access on a private record, which requires programmatic Apex managed sharing; a trigger inserts an EmployeeReview share row at access level Edit for that user. Populating a lookup does not grant access (A), workflow cannot create share records (B), and a criteria-based rule cannot target the arbitrary user in the lookup (D).

question: Assuming granular locking is enabled, what activity can happen in parallel to changing a community account owner without risking group membership lock errors?
optionA: Deletion of a Role
optionB: Creation of a Role
optionC: Creation of a Territory
optionD: Deletion of a Territory
explanation: With granular locking, operations affecting non-overlapping parts of the group-membership graph can run in parallel, so creating a Territory does not conflict with changing a community account owner and avoids a group-membership lock error. Role creation or deletion and Territory deletion touch overlapping membership structures and can contend.

question: A banking company wants their customers Date of Birth Field searchable by Banking Reps, but only editable by Customer Support Reps. Which approach is recommended to meet this requirement?
optionA: Create a Validation rule on the Date of Birth field so the rule returns true only when user.profilename matches Customer Support Rep.
optionB: Set the Field-Level Security for the Date of Birth field to be Visible to the Customer Support Rep profile, and set the Date of Birth field Visible and Read-Only to the Banking Rep profile.
optionC: Add Date of Birth field to the Search layout of the Contact Object. Modify the Page layout assigned to Customer Support Rep and add Date of Birth field as Required.
explanation: Field-Level Security is the declarative way to make the Date of Birth field searchable and visible to Banking Reps as read-only while keeping it editable for Customer Support Reps. A validation rule (A) blocks save but does not cleanly control visibility and edit, and requiring the field on a layout (C) does not restrict who may edit it.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: When you make changes to roles and groups Salesforce locks the entire group membership table, which makes it impossible to process group changes in multiple threads to increase throughput on updates.
explanation: The described whole-table group-membership lock that blocks multi-threaded processing is exactly what Granular Locking solves; it locks only the affected membership subsets so updates can proceed in parallel and increase throughput. This is the platform feature that removes the single-table bottleneck.

question: A user at Universal Containers would like to give access to a Report folder to someone else in the organization. Which two access permissions should the user have to support this requirement? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: The "Viewer" Report folder permission
optionB: The "Editor" Report folder permission.
optionC: The "Manage Reports in Public Folders" Profile permission.
optionD: The "Manager" Report folder permission.
explanation: Giving someone else access to a report folder requires the "Manage Reports in Public Folders" profile permission and the folder-level "Manager" access that allows sharing the folder. The Viewer and Editor folder roles (A and B) can use or edit content but cannot grant other users access.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a business unit that uses a custom object to track requests which demands a high level of internal team collaboration. Although all requests must have an owner, all employees are required to collaborate on requests. Which recommendation should a Salesforce architect provide to optimize the solution and meet these requirements?
optionA: Set the Request object's OWD to Public Read/Write.
optionB: Set the Request object's OWD to Private and grant Modify All Data permission on all Profiles for the Request object.
optionC: Set the Request object's OWD to Private and criteria-based sharing rule to share all Request records with all internal users.
optionD: Set the Request object's OWD to Public Read Only and configure role hierarchy.
explanation: When every employee must read and edit requests while each record still has an owner, the simplest optimal model is OWD Public Read/Write, which retains ownership but lets all users collaborate. Modify All Data (B) over-grants across the platform, and a rule sharing to all internal users (C) is heavier than simply opening the OWD.

question: Which two access grants are stored in the Group Maintenance tables? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Inherited access grants
optionB: Explicit grants
optionC: Group Membership grants
optionD: Implicit grants
explanation: The Group Maintenance tables store group-membership grants (C) and the inherited access derived from role-hierarchy and group-nesting relationships (A). Explicit and implicit grants (B and D) are stored in the object share and access tables, not in the group maintenance tables.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has 200 distributors that use Partner Community Licenses. Partners cannot see each other's data, but UC is also trying to give more visibility to certain individuals at a distributor. Which scalable solution would an architect recommend that will give users in the partner manager role access to all Case and Container records owned by other partner managers and partner users (but not the partner executive) at the same distributor?
optionA: Create Sharing sets.
optionB: Create a permission set granting the View All permission to Case and Container records.
optionC: Give Super User permission to the partner manager users.
optionD: Create ownership-based sharing rules for your distributors.
explanation: Super User permission gives a partner manager access to records owned by users at their role and below at the same distributor, which includes other partner managers and partner users but excludes the partner executive above them, and it scales across 200 distributors without per-distributor rules. Ownership-based rules for each distributor (D, the source answer) do not scale to that many distributors, and a View All permission set (B) over-grants. This is consistent with Q128 and Q135.

question: Universal Containers has requirement to integrate Salesforce with an external system to control record access. What option should the Architect consider when designing a solution?
optionA: Use the Security API to modify the role hierarchy in Salesforce.
optionB: Use the SOAP API to maintain the related SObject_share records.
optionC: Use the Metadata API to maintain the related SObject records.
optionD: Use the SOAP API to create new Criteria-Based Sharing Rules in Salesforce.
explanation: An external integration that controls record access maintains the share tables directly, which is done through the SOAP API on the object's share records such as AccountShare. The Metadata API (C) handles configuration and metadata rather than data rows, and you cannot create per-record criteria sharing rules through the API for this need (D).

question: The sales manager in Japan have asked the sales manager in Australia to assist them with closing their deals. How are these requirements achieved?
optionA: Use sharing set to give the sales manager access to the deals.
optionB: Create ownership-based sharing rule.
optionC: Use opportunity teams to automatically add the sales manager as a team member.
optionD: Assign the sales manager View All on the opportunity object.
explanation: The Australia manager needs access to the Japan team's deals, so an ownership-based sharing rule sharing opportunities owned by the Japan managers to the Australia manager grants it declaratively. A sharing set (A) is community-only and View All (D) over-grants. Opportunity teams (C) are arguably cleaner for purely ad-hoc help, so this is worth noting.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a private Organization-Wide Defaults (OWD) model for the Account object and needs to control the access of records and fields according to these requirements: * Sales reps can view/edit only their own records and cannot access the field "segment" in account page. * Service reps need to view all accounts but cannot edit any information. * Sales managers can modify any account of reps reporting to them. * Service managers can modify any account. How can a Salesforce architect provide an optimal solution for this scenario?
optionA: Use profiles, sharing rules, and change OWD to public read-only.
optionB: Use profiles, manual sharing, and field-level security.
optionC: Use profiles, permission sets, and field-level security.
optionD: Use profiles, permission sets, role hierarchy, and field-level security.
explanation: The mixed requirements of own versus all, edit versus view, manager-modifies-reports, and hiding a field require the full stack: profiles and permission sets for object access, role hierarchy for the manager-edit rule, and Field-Level Security to hide the segment field. Options that omit role hierarchy or FLS (A, B, and C) cannot satisfy both the manager-edit and the field-hiding requirements.

question: Universal Containers regularly uploads large amounts of parent and child records into Salesforce to maintain integrations with other systems that update their date in scheduled batches or continuously in real time. Which two situations may pose a risk of producing locking errors? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Updates to child records that have the same parent records are being processed simultaneously in separate transactions.
optionB: Updates to parent and child records are being processed in the same batch.
optionC: Updates to parent records and their child records being processed simultaneously in separate threads.
optionD: Updates to parent and child records are being processed synchronously in the same threads.
explanation: Lock contention arises when related parent and child rows are updated concurrently in separate transactions or threads, because Salesforce locks the parent during the child's sharing and rollup operations. Processing parent and child together in the same batch or synchronously in the same thread (B and D) serializes the work and avoids the lock.

question: A dummy user at Universal Containers owns more that 10,000 lead records. The system assigned all these leads to a dummy user. This is causing performance issues whenever role hierarchy changes. Which two options should be recommended to improve performance? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Assign ownership to a small number of users.
optionB: Add the dummy user into a public group.
optionC: Add the dummy user to the bottom of the role hierarchy.
optionD: Do not assign a role to the dummy user.
explanation: A dummy user owning more than 10,000 leads creates ownership skew that slows role-hierarchy recalculation, so distributing ownership across many users (A) and removing the dummy user's role (D) so its records are excluded from hierarchy recalculation both relieve it. Adding the user to a public group (B) or to the bottom of the hierarchy (C) does not remove the skew or its recalculation cost.

question: Universal Container (UC) wants all full-time internal employees to be able to view all leads. A subset of employees should also be able to see leads. Which organization default (OWD) approach should an architect recommend that will help US implement these requirement?
optionA: Implement a Public Read/write OWD on Lead
optionB: Implement a Public Read only OWD on Lead
optionC: Implement a Public Read/write/Transfer OWD on Lead.
optionD: Implement a Private OWD on Lead.
explanation: All employees must view all leads while a subset can also edit, so Public Read Only gives universal visibility and sharing rules then grant edit to the subset. A Private model (D) would hide leads, and Read/Write or Read/Write/Transfer (A and C) would over-grant edit rights to everyone.

question: Universal Containers has set Account Sharing to Private with Account Teams enabled. Which two user groups can add team members on the Account? Choose 2 answers
optionA: The current Account Owner can add team members.
optionB: The user specified as the Manager on the Owner's User record
optionC: Someone above the Account Owner in the Role Hierarchy with read access
optionD: Any Account Team Member with read access on the Account
explanation: Account Team members can be added by the current account owner (A) and by users above the owner in the Role Hierarchy who have read access to the account (C). The owner's "Manager" user-record field (B) is irrelevant to sharing, and existing team members themselves (D) cannot add others.
