question: Universal Containers has the following requirements: * The Commercial Account and Consumer Account support departments should not collaborate. * The Commercial and Consumer sales users roll up to the same VP of Sales, but there should be no collaboration between sales departments. * The Commercial sales department should share its customers with the Commercial support department. * The Consumer sales department shares its customers with the Consumer support department. * The Commercial and Consumer support departments roll up to the same Support Director. * The sales departments will remain the Account Owner for the Accounts that they sell to. What is the recommended Org-Wide Sharing Default for Accounts, and how would the Architect enable proper Commercial and Consumer Sales to Support Account Sharing for this scenario?
optionA: Private Account Sharing with Sharing Rules from Commercial Sales Role(s) to Consumer Support Role(s) and Consumer Sales Role(s) to Commercial Support Role(s).
optionB: Private Account Sharing with Sharing Rules from Commercial support Role(s) to Commercial Support Role(s) and Consumer Sales Role(s) to Consumer Support Role(s).
optionC: Read-Only Account Sharing with Sharing Rules from Commercial Sales Role(s) to Consumer Support Group(s) and Consumer Sales Role(s) to Commercial Support Groups(s).
optionD: Private Account Sharing with Sharing Rules from Commercial Sales Group(s) to Commercial Support Groups(s) and Consumer Sales Group(s) to Consumer Support Group(s).
explanation: The sales departments stay owners under a Private model, and group-to-group sharing rules mapping each Sales group to its matching Support group give each support team only its own department's accounts with no cross-collaboration. The cross-matched mappings (A and C) would share to the wrong department, and B's role-based wording does not correctly isolate the departments.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has created a public group with certain Sales Engineers to help on complex deals and a sharing rule to grant access to these opportunities. Opportunity OWD is private. What is the impact of these sharing settings?
optionA: Subordinates of Managers who have Sales Engineers in the public group will also have access to these records.
optionB: Sales Engineers that have a similar role of the Sales Engineers of the public group will also have access to these records.
optionC: Sales Engineers Managers and their managers in the role hierarchy will also have access to these records.
optionD: Sales Engineers direct reports will also have access to these records.
explanation: Sharing to a public group grants access to the group's members and, through the Role Hierarchy, to users above those members, so the Sales Engineers' managers and their managers also gain access to the opportunities. Subordinates, similar-role peers, and direct reports (A, B, and D) do not inherit a group-based share.

question: Universal Containers has recently activated an integration that synchronizes customer information and orders into their CRM of choice: Salesforce.com. One of their largest customers, United Air, has over 12,000 unique contacts. Since the integration was activated, sales reps are having trouble adding contacts to the United Air account. When a sales rep adds a contact, they get the following error message: UNABLE_TO_LOCK_ROW, this issue seems to affect the United Air account and several other large customers. The sales rep can usually save the contact by trying again later in the evening. What should the Architect recommend as a possible solution?
optionA: Add a role-based sharing rule so all sales team members have Read/Write access to contacts.
optionB: Remove sharing rules and replace them with Apex sharing for Unite Air and the other large accounts.
optionC: Implement an account hierarchy and redistribute the contacts evenly under the child accounts.
optionD: Create a permission set for the sales team to grant them Read/Write access to all account fields.
explanation: The UNABLE_TO_LOCK_ROW error on a 12,000-contact account is parent (lookup) data skew, where too many child contacts under one account cause lock contention; redistributing the contacts across child accounts in an account hierarchy relieves it. Sharing-rule changes (A and B) or a field-access permission set (D) do not address the parent-row locking.

question: At Universal Containers, users should only see Accounts they or their subordinates own. All Accounts with the custom field "Kay Customer" should be visible to all Senior Account Managers. There is a custom field on the Account record that contains sensitive information and should be hidden from all users, except 3 designated users who require view and edit access. These three users come from different user groups, and will change occasionally. Which three platform security features are required to support these requirements with the minimum amount of effort? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Criteria-Based Sharing Rules
optionB: Owner-Based Sharing Rules
optionC: Role Hierarchy
optionD: Apex Managed Sharing
explanation: The owner-or-subordinates visibility comes from the Role Hierarchy under a Private model (C), the Key Customer visibility for all Senior Account Managers is a criteria-based sharing rule (A), and the sensitive field for three rotating cross-group users is best handled with a Permission Set granting FLS (E). Owner-based rules (B) and Apex managed sharing (D) add unnecessary effort for these requirements.

question: Universal Containers is a fast-growing company that sells containers globally. It has thousands of dealerships throughout the world where local dealers service Containers sold locally. They recently opened two dealerships in California: NorthCal and SoCal. Universal Containers implemented a new partner community to enable their dealers. Each dealership has a dealer Manager who has all service agents report into them. Assuming a private sharing model, what is the best option to enable dealer managers to have visibility to customer cases within their dealership and not across all dealerships?
optionA: Create sharing groups that share all cases to all agents under the Dealer manager.
optionB: Create a batch job that creates sharing rules as needed, based on the cases created.
optionC: Build a trigger that create manual sharing of cases as needed whenever a new case is created.
optionD: No changes are needed to the sharing and visibility model to implement this requirement.
explanation: In a private partner community, the dealer manager already sits above their service agents in the Role Hierarchy per dealership, so cases owned by agents are visible to their own manager and not across dealerships with no extra configuration. Sharing groups, batch jobs, or triggers (A, B, and C) are redundant given the hierarchy.

question: Sales Reps at Universal Containers sometimes create large files as a part of the Sales process that are too large to share over email. They would like users to be able to share files with customers, but the CISO has requested that any file links shared must be protected by a password. How can this be accomplished?
optionA: Set up an experience cloud site for customers to access files. Share the file with customers via chatter. Customers can then log into the site to access the content.
optionB: Sales reps can create a content delivery. During creation, the user should select the option to require a password to access content.
optionC: Utilize an AppExchange product for delivering password-protected files to their customers,
explanation: Salesforce Content Delivery lets a rep generate a shareable link for a large file and, at creation, require a password to access it, meeting the CISO's requirement natively. An Experience Cloud site (A) is heavier and a site login is not a per-file password, and an AppExchange product (C) is unnecessary for a built-in capability.

question: A sales rep at Universal Containers (UC) has manually shared an Opportunity record with internal Pre-Sales users. After some time, the sales rep moved to another position and all opportunities records that were owned were transferred to a new sales rep. What happened to the internal Pre-Sales users' access to the opportunity?
optionA: They still have access to the record due to inherited sharing.
optionB: They still have access to the record due to implicit sharing.
optionC: They will no longer have access to the record.
optionD: They still have access to the record due to team access.
explanation: A manual share is deleted when record ownership changes, so when the opportunity is transferred to a new rep the Pre-Sales users' manually granted access is removed. That access is not preserved by implicit, inherited, or team mechanisms (A, B, and D), because none of those created the share.

question: Universal Containers would like to create a custom team solution that can be used on a custom Loan object. The following requirements must be met: The Loan custom object should be set to Private in the Org-Wide Defaults. Any user added to the Custom Team object should have Read Only access to the corresponding Loan record. If the Custom Team record is marked as "Primary" then the corresponding user should have Read/Edit access to the corresponding Loan record. Which two methods will allow the Architect to meet the requirements? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Create a custom trigger on the Custom Team object that inserts or updates records in the Loan_share object.
optionB: Create a criteria-based sharing rule on the Loan object that will share the Loan record with the appropriate user in the Custom Team object.
optionC: Create Apex Sharing Reasons on the Loan object to identify the reason the Loan record was share.
optionD: Create an owner-based sharing rule on the Custom Team object that will share the Loan record to the owner of the Custom Team record.
explanation: Differential access of Read for members and Read/Edit for the Primary record on a private object requires Apex managed sharing: a trigger writes Loan share rows at the correct access level (A), and defining Apex Sharing Reasons identifies and maintains those shares and preserves them on owner change (C). A criteria-based rule (B) cannot read the related Custom Team's Primary flag per record, and an owner-based rule (D) shares to the wrong target.

question: Universal Containers would like to restrict users' access to export reports What option supports this requirement?
optionA: Remove the "Report Manager" user permission.
optionB: Remove the Export button from the report page layout.
optionC: Remove "Allow Export" on the report folder settings.
optionD: Remove the "Export Reports" profile permission.
explanation: Restricting report export is governed by the "Export Reports" profile permission, so removing it stops users from exporting. Reports have no page layouts, so an Export button on a report page layout (B, the source answer) does not exist, and there is no "Allow Export" folder setting (C). This corrects the source.

question: Which two reasons should the Architect consider regarding the use of Apex Sharing Reasons? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Ensuring the developer can more easily troubleshoot programmatic sharing.
optionB: Ensuring the Share record is not deleted upon ownership change.
optionC: Ensuring the Share record is deleted upon ownership change.
optionD: Ensuring there is additional criteria available for Criteria-Based Sharing.
explanation: Apex Sharing Reasons label programmatic shares so developers can identify and troubleshoot why a record was shared (A), and shares created with a custom reason are retained when ownership changes, unlike Manual shares (B). They do not delete shares on owner change (C) and do not add criteria to criteria-based sharing (D).

question: Universal Containers has Controlled by Parent sharing settings on the Contact object and Public Read Only sharing settings on Account, Opportunity, and Case objects. Which two options can the user see while adding Account team members to the Account?
optionA: Case Access
optionB: Opportunity Access
optionC: Contact Access
optionD: Activity Access
explanation: When adding Account team members, access levels appear for child objects whose OWD is more restrictive than Public Read/Write, and here Case and Opportunity are Public Read Only so their access levels are shown. Contact is Controlled by Parent so it has no independent setting, and Activity is not an account-team access option.

question: (Variant) Universal Containers' organization-wide-defaults model is Private for the Account object. A sales rep has access to opportunity records. Which level of access will the sales rep have to the related Account record?
optionA: No access
optionB: Read/Create/Edit access
optionC: Read/Create access
optionD: Read-only access
explanation: (Variant of Q18.) With the Account OWD Private, a sales rep who has access to a related opportunity gains implicit Read-only access to the parent account. The rep never receives create or edit on the parent (B and C), and it is not no access (A).

question: Universal Container is creating a custom VF page to allow user to edit contact records. The developer has used an apex:outputField for the Phone field on the contact obj. What is the expected behavior if a user without FLS accesses the VF page?
optionA: The user encounters an error while saving the record
optionB: The field is automatically removed from the page
optionC: The output field is visible to user
optionD: The user is able to enter a value into the phone field
explanation: An apex:outputField automatically enforces Field-Level Security, so a user without access to the Phone field simply does not see it; Visualforce removes the field rather than throwing an error. It will not error on save (A), remain visible (C), or allow data entry (D).
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers (UC) service reps are assigned to a profile which has View All on the Case object (Private OWD). To make sure service reps have access to all relevant information to attend to customer requests, which two details should Salesforce Architects consider? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Service reps will be able to access all UC contact records if they are controlled by parent.
optionB: Service reps will not be able to access all UC contact records if they are controlled by parent.
optionC: Service reps will be able to access all the UC Account records due to Implicit Sharing.
optionD: Service reps will not be able to access all the UC Account records because Account OWD is private.
explanation: View All on the Case object does not extend to other objects, so service reps still cannot see Accounts because the Account OWD is Private (D), and they cannot see Contacts that are Controlled by Parent because access is gated by the inaccessible parent accounts (B). Implicit sharing does not grant account access from Case View All, so A and C are incorrect.

question: For the Universal Containers Commercial and Consumer support departments, having access to Activities for Contacts with which they interact is important. Commercial support users should not see Consumer Accounts/Contacts and Consumer support users should not see Commercial Accounts/Contacts. Assuming the Organization-Wide Default for Activities is set to "Controlled by Parent" what is the minimum level of Sharing access a support user would need to Accounts/Contacts to view associated Activities?
optionA: Private Account/Contact Sharing Default with a Sharing Rule for each department set to Public Read/Write access to Accounts/Contacts.
optionB: Private Account/Contact Sharing Default with a Sharing Rule for each department set to Private access to Accounts/Contacts.
optionC: The users need no access to Accounts/Contacts with the proper Activity Sharing Rules and Profile Permissions for the Accounts Tab.
optionD: Private Account/Contact Sharing Default with a Sharing Rule for each department set to Public Read only access to Accounts/Contacts.
explanation: With Activities set to Controlled by Parent, a user needs at least Read on the parent Account or Contact to see its activities, so keep Accounts and Contacts Private and grant each department Read-only through sharing rules to keep commercial and consumer data separated. Read/Write (A) over-grants, and "no access" to accounts and contacts (C) cannot satisfy Controlled-by-Parent activity visibility.

question: The Architect at Universal Containers has created a List View to show all open Opportunities that were created in the last month, and would like to make this list view visible to certain groups of users. Which two options are available to the Architect for sharing the List View?
optionA: Public Groups
optionB: Profiles
optionC: Roles and Subordinates
optionD: Manual Sharing
explanation: A list view can be shared with public groups (A) or with roles and subordinates (C), both of which scope visibility to a defined set of users. List views cannot be shared to profiles (B) or through manual per-record sharing (D).

question: Universal containers (UC) implemented a private organization-wide default for the Container and Case objects. How can UC give support representatives access to Container and Case records owned by Customer Community users?
optionA: Create an ownership-based sharing rule that gives access to the head of support role and internal subordinates
optionB: Create a criteria-based sharing rule that gives access to the head of support role and internal subordinates
optionC: Support representatives will automatically get access to these records via the role hierarchy.
optionD: Create a Share Group based on the sharing set created for the Customer Community User Profile.
explanation: Records owned by high-volume Customer Community users are not accessible to internal users by default, so creating a Share Group on that profile's Sharing Set grants internal support reps access to those community-owned Container and Case records. Ownership- or criteria-based sharing rules (A and B) and the role hierarchy (C) do not reach records owned by community users.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is implementing Sales Cloud. During the last quarter of the financial year, they requested a solution in Salesforce to allow them to specify an assistance agent on the opportunity via an assistant field. The system should automatically remove access from the previous assistant when the field changes. What is the optimum solution to meet the requirements?
optionA: Use share group to share opportunities with the assistant agent.
optionB: Use opportunity team and create an assistant field, use apex to share opportunities with the assistant
optionC: Use sharing rule to share opportunities with the assistant agent.
optionD: Use apex sharing to share and unicast opportunities with the assistant agent.
explanation: The requirement is to set an assistant on the opportunity and automatically remove the previous assistant's access when the field changes, and Apex managed sharing is the only option that can both grant the new assistant's share and programmatically delete the prior one. Opportunity teams (B, the source answer) do not auto-remove a prior member on a field change, and a static sharing rule (C) cannot revoke access. The source question text was garbled, so this is a best-guess reconstruction.

question: Which two options are available to share a Report or Dashboard folder with other users in the Organization? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Profiles
optionB: Public Groups
optionC: Roles
optionD: Teams
explanation: Report and dashboard folders can be shared with public groups (B) and roles (C), among other targets. Profiles (A) and Teams (D) are not valid folder-sharing targets.

question: Universal Containers is implementing a community of High-Volume Community users. Community users should be able to see records associated to their Account or Contact record. The Architect is planning to use a Sharing Set to provide access to the records. When setting up the Sharing Set, certain objects are not available in the list of Available Objects. Which two reasons explain why an object is excluded from the list of Available Objects in a Sharing Set?
optionA: The object is a custom object, and therefore not available for a sharing set.
optionB: The object's Organization-Wide sharing setting is set to Public Read/Write.
optionC: The object's Organization-Wide sharing setting is set to Private.
optionD: The custom object does not have a lookup to Accounts or Contacts.
explanation: Sharing Sets appear only for objects whose access can be extended, so an object already set to Public Read/Write does not need one and is excluded (B), and a custom object must have a lookup to Account or Contact to be eligible (D). Custom objects are not categorically excluded (A), and Private objects (C) are exactly what sharing sets are designed to extend.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a requirement to expose a web service to their business partners. The web service will be used to allow each business partner to query UC's Salesforce instance to retrieve the status of orders. The business partner should only be allowed access to orders for which the business partner is the fulfillment vendor. The Architect does not want the business partners to utilize the standard APIs and would prefer a custom API be developed. Which three design elements should the Architect consider in order to ensure the data security of the solution? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Query the Orders object with Dynamic SOQL based upon the fulfillment ID.
optionB: Set the Orders object's sharing settings to Private in the Org-Wide Defaults
optionC: Provide each partner with their own Salesforce login set to API Enabled on the profile.
optionD: Develop a custom Apex web service with a fulfillment ID input attribute
optionE: Develop a custom Apex web service using the "With Sharing" keyword.
explanation: Data security for the partner web service comes from a Private Orders OWD (B), a "with sharing" Apex service that respects the running user's access (E), and filtering the SOQL by the partner's fulfillment ID (A). Per-partner logins (C) and a fulfillment-ID input parameter (D) do not themselves enforce row-level security and could be spoofed.

question: Universal Containers has Public Read Only sharing settings on the Opportunity object. What Opportunity access options can the user see while adding account team members to the Account?
optionA: Read Only
optionB: Private and Read Only
optionC: Private
optionD: Read Only and Read/Write
explanation: When the Opportunity OWD is Public Read Only, the account-team Opportunity access dropdown offers only the levels above that baseline, namely Read Only and Read/Write. Private is not selectable because you cannot drop access below the OWD, which makes A, B, and C incorrect.

question: Universal Containers has the following requirements: A custom Loan object requires Org-Wide Defaults set to Private. The owner of the Loan record will be the Loan Origination Officer. The Loan record must be shared with a specific Underwriter on a loan-by-loan basis. The Underwriters should only see the Loan records for which they are assigned. What should the Architect recommend to meet these requirements?
optionA: Use criteria-based sharing rules to share the Loan object with the Underwriter based upon the criteria defined in the criteria-based sharing
optionB: Create a lookup relationship from the Loan object to the User object. Use a trigger on the Loan object to create the corresponding record in the Loan share object
optionC: Create a master-detail relationship from the Loan to the User object. Loan records will be automatically shared with the Underwriter
optionD: Create an Apex Sharing Reason on the Loan object that shares the Loan with the Underwriter based upon the criteria defined in the Sharing Reason
explanation: Per-loan assignment to a specific underwriter, who is an arbitrary user, requires Apex managed sharing: add a User lookup on the Loan and a trigger that inserts the corresponding Loan share row for the assigned underwriter. A master-detail to User (C) is not allowed and would not auto-share, and a criteria-based rule (A) cannot target an arbitrary per-record user.

question: Universal Containers (UC) implemented Sales Cloud and requested that only certain branch staff trained to sell high risk products can create opportunities for high risk products. In which two ways can an Architect allow only specific branch staff to sell high risk products? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Set Price Book Organization Wide Default to View Only and share the price book (High Risk) with the trained staff.
optionB: Configure Price Book Organization wide default to No Access.
optionC: Share (High Risk) price book with the trained staff via manual sharing.
optionD: Share (High Risk) price book with the trained staff via sharing rule.
explanation: Access to sell specific products is controlled through price book sharing: set the Price Book OWD to View Only so not everyone gets the High Risk book (A), and share the High Risk price book with the trained staff, for example through manual sharing (C). Setting OWD to No Access (B) breaks all price book use, and price books cannot be shared through sharing rules (D).

question: In order to allow community users to collaborate on Opportunities, which license type must the users.
optionA: Sales Community
optionB: Customer Community
optionC: Partner Community
optionD: Customer Community plus
explanation: Collaborating on Opportunities requires a license that supports the Opportunity object together with roles, which is Partner Community. Customer Community and Customer Community Plus are oriented to support and self-service and do not provide partner opportunity collaboration in the same way.

question: Universal Containers (UC) uses a custom Visualforce page to display shipment tracking information (custom object, private OWD) to field manager and agents. The IT team wants to make sure that users have access to only the information that is allowed. Which Apex method must be used to make sure only allowed fields are shown to the users?
optionA: isReadable()
optionB: isShowable()
optionC: isAccessible()
optionD: isViewable()
explanation: To ensure only permitted fields display on a Visualforce or Apex page, use DescribeFieldResult.isAccessible() to check field-level read access before rendering each field. isReadable, isShowable, and isViewable are not the Schema methods that check Field-Level Security.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Which two objects support creating queues? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Account.
optionB: Opportunity.
optionC: Lead.
optionD: Case.
explanation: Queues are supported on Lead (C) and Case (D), among a few other objects such as custom objects and Orders. Account and Opportunity do not support queues, so A and B are incorrect.

question: Universal Containers has a Private Sharing Model and restrictive data access settings in place. The data governance team is planning to deploy a master data management tool that will need write access to all system data, and the Architect has been asked to prepare a Data Governance User Profile that has full access to all data regardless of Sharing and CRUD Settings. What is the optimal way to enable this requirement?
optionA: Ensure the profile has the "Modify All Data permission enabled
optionB: Create a Sharing Rule for required Objects, on an API Only profile
optionC: Create an All-Data Access app as a default for this Profile.
optionD: Enable the "Edit Read -Only Fields" permission for this Profile.
explanation: A profile that needs full read and write access to all data regardless of sharing and CRUD is granted the "Modify All Data" permission, which bypasses sharing and object-level permissions. A sharing rule (B) does not bypass CRUD, and an app default (C) or the "Edit Read-Only Fields" permission (D) do not grant universal data access.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Mary is Joe's manager in the role hierarchy. The OWD for a custom Invoice object is Public ReadOnly and Mary's profile is not granted the Read permission for the Invoice object. What action can Mary take on Joe's Invoice records?
optionA: Read/Write
optionB: Edit Only
optionC: None
optionD: View Only
explanation: The OWD is Public Read Only, but Mary's profile lacks Read on the Invoice object, and object-level CRUD gates everything, so the Role Hierarchy cannot grant access to an object she has no permission for. As a result she has no access, rather than Read/Write, Edit Only, or View Only.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: What is the best practice for testing sharing and visibility changes?
optionA: Use Administrative and User reports to view the Active Users.
optionB: Use the Login As feature for a sample user in each role and profile.
optionC: Use Field Audit Trail to audit the field meta-data and visibility.
optionD: Use the Sharing button to test Profile and Permission set changes.
explanation: The best practice for verifying sharing and visibility changes is to use the Login As feature for a representative user in each role and profile and confirm what they can actually see. Reports of active users (A), Field Audit Trail (C), and the Sharing button (D) do not validate end-user record visibility holistically.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a custom object to track the internal net promoter score (NPS) for all .. How can UC ensure that NPS records cannot be accessed by an individual employee's manager?
optionA: Create a criteria-based sharing rule to remove access to Manager role and above in the Role ..
optionB: Remove Create, Read, Edit and Delete from Manager Profiles and Permission sets.
optionC: Use Apex Sharing to remove NPS object share records for Manager profiles.
optionD: Set organization wide default to Private and uncheck the Access Using Hierarchies option for the NPS object
explanation: To ensure an employee's manager cannot see NPS records, set the object to Private and disable "Grant Access Using Hierarchies" so managers do not inherit their subordinates' records. A criteria-based rule "to remove access" (A) is not how sharing works, since sharing only grants, and removing CRUD (B) would block legitimate users too.

question: To grant Universal Containers sales managers access to shipment records it was necessary to leverage Apex managed sharing, and the IT team is worried about improper access to records. Which two features and best practices should a Salesforce architect recommend to mitigate the risk?
optionA: Use isShareable keyword in Apex classes to assure record visibility will be followed.
optionB: Use runAs system method in test classes to test using different users and profiles.
optionC: Use with Sharing keyword in Apex classes to assure record visibility will be followed
optionD: Use isAccessible keyword in Apex classes to assure record visibility will be followed.
explanation: To mitigate improper access from Apex managed sharing, write Apex with the "with sharing" keyword so the running user's record visibility is enforced (C), and test with runAs() across different users and profiles (B). The "isShareable" and "isAccessable" keywords (A and D) are not valid Apex constructs.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Which users have access to Opportunity records owned by an external user, assuming no other changes to the sharing model have been implemented?
optionA: The record owner and any user above the external user in the role hierarchy.
optionB: The record owner only.
optionC: The record owner and any user below the external user in the role hierarchy.
optionD: The record owner and all internal users.
explanation: An opportunity owned by an external (community) user is, by default, visible to that owner and to internal users above the external user's role in the hierarchy, because the role hierarchy rolls access upward. It is not limited to the owner alone (B), to subordinates (C), or to all internal users (D).

question: Universal Containers is updating its Organization-Wide Sharing Settings for the Account Object from a "Public Read/Write" model to a "Private" model, so that they can hide certain national accounts from sales reps and sales managers. These national accounts should only be accessible by sales directors and above. Universal Container's Role Hierarchy matches its organizational hierarchy. Which two options should the Architect consider when designing the solution? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Sales directors will need a sharing rule created so that they can see accounts owned by Sales Users.
optionB: National accounts must be owned by a user who is above the sales managers in the Role Hierarchy.
optionC: Apex managed sharing will have to be disabled for the account object to protect the national accounts.
optionD: If a sales rep is added to the Opportunity Team for a national account, they will gain access to account data.
explanation: To hide national accounts from reps and managers under a Private model, those accounts must be owned by someone above the managers in the hierarchy so the managers do not inherit them (B), and note the side effect that adding a rep to a national account's Opportunity Team grants implicit access to the account (D). Sales directors do not need a special rule to see subordinate-owned accounts (A, since the hierarchy handles it), and disabling Apex sharing (C) is not required.

question: Universal Containers has junction object called "Job Production Facility", with 2 master-detail relationships to the Job and Production Facility custom objects. Both master records have a private sharing model. What statement is true if a user's profile allows access (Create/Read) to the Job, but no access to the Production Facility Object?
optionA: The user will be granted read access to the Production Facility record
optionB: The user will see the junction object and the link to the Production Facility.
optionC: The user will see the junction object but not the link to the Production Facility.
optionD: The user will not be able to see the junction object records or the field values.
explanation: A junction object's record access requires access to both master records, so lacking any access to the Production Facility object means the user cannot see the junction records at all. They therefore do not gain read on the parent (A) and do not see the junction with or without the link (B and C).

question: A developer at Universal Container is building an integration within a managed package for their internal org that requires login to an external system. The end point requires basic authentication. The Architect would like to ensure that the username and password are managed securely. Which three options should the Architect recommend to secure the credentials?
optionA: Store the credentials in a custom object using encrypted fields
optionB: Store the credentials in named credentials that are used in the apex callout
optionC: Store the credentials in the apex code, which will not be available to non-admins
optionD: Store the credentials in protected custom metadata that are used in the apex callout
optionE: Store the credentials in protected custom settings that are used in the apex callout
explanation: Basic-auth credentials in a managed package are secured through named credentials, which store and inject the credentials securely (B), or through protected custom metadata (D) or protected custom settings (E) that subscribers cannot view. An encrypted field on a custom object (A) and hardcoding in Apex (C) are not secure storage for callout credentials.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Sales managers want their team members to help each other close Opportunities. The Opportunity and Account organization-wide defaults are private. To grant Opportunity access to sales reps on the same team, owner ship-based sharing rules were created for each team. What is the side effect of this approach?
optionA: All sales reps will have Read access to Accounts for all Opportunities.
optionB: Sales Reps on the same team will have Edit access to the Accounts for Opportunities owned by then team members.
optionC: Sales reps on the same team will have Read access to the Accounts for Opportunities owned by their team members.
optionD: All sales reps will have Read access to all Accounts.
explanation: Ownership-based opportunity sharing rules grant implicit Read access to the parent Account of those opportunities, so team members get Read, not Edit, on those specific accounts rather than on all accounts. That is why the all-accounts options (A and D) and the Edit option (B) are incorrect.

question: The architect at Universal Containers would like to prevent users from editing encrypted fields. Assuming no customizations are implemented, which two options should the Architect choose to support the requirement? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Validation Rules
optionB: Page Layout settings
optionC: Apex Triggers
optionD: Workflow Rules
explanation: Without custom code, you prevent editing of encrypted fields by using a validation rule that blocks changes (A) or by setting the field to read-only on the page layout (B). Triggers (C) are customization, and workflow rules (D) act after the record is saved rather than preventing the edit.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers wants to store Payment Term Details on the Account object, but the fields should only be visible on certain record types and for certain user profiles. How can a System Administrator quickly determine which user profiles, page layouts, and record types include certain fields?
optionA: Log in as each user profile and view the Account Page Layouts.
optionB: Use the Field Accessibility Viewer for the fields in question
optionC: Universally require the field at the field level.
optionD: Click the Field-Level Security for the field on each Profile.
explanation: The Field Accessibility viewer shows, in a single matrix, which profiles, record types, and page layouts expose a field as visible, hidden, or read-only, making it the fastest way to audit field exposure. Logging in as each user (A) or checking FLS per profile (D) is slow, and universally requiring the field (C) is irrelevant to the audit.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers has implemented a community for its customers using the Customer Community sense type. They have implemented a custom object to store service requests that has a look up to the account record. The Organization Wide Default External Access for the service request object is set to Private. Universal Containers wants their customers to be able to see service requests for their account through the community Customers should not see service requests for other accounts. What Salesforce feature can the Architect use to implement this?
optionA: Use manual sharing to share the service requests manually when a new community user is added.
optionB: Use a Sharing Set to share service requests related to the account based on the community user's profile.
optionC: Use a Sharing Rule to share service requests to the community user based on their role.
optionD: Use Apex Managed Sharing to share service requests related to the account to the appropriate community users.
explanation: Customer Community users have no roles, so a Sharing Set keyed on the service request's lookup to the user's Account grants each customer access to their account's service requests and nothing else. Manual sharing (A), role-based sharing rules (C), and Apex managed sharing (D) are unnecessary overhead compared with the declarative Sharing Set.

question: Universal Containers has set the Org-Wide Sharing Default for Accounts to Private and has created some sharing rules to extend access based on certain data access policies. An architect has been asked to review access to a certain set of key customer accounts. How might this review be conducted?
optionA: Export the Account Share table and review.
optionB: Run a Report on Sharing in the Admin Console.
optionC: Log in as each user and Run the All Accounts List View.
optionD: Use the Sharing button on each customer Account.
explanation: To audit who has access to a specific set of accounts under a Private model with sharing rules, export and inspect the AccountShare table, which lists every share row and its row cause. Logging in as each user (C) or using the per-record Sharing button (D) does not scale for a set of accounts, and there is no built-in sharing report in an admin console (B).

question: Universal Containers has a custom Job object with a private sharing model. Based on the size and location of the Job, different teams must have access to edit the specific Job record. To support this requirement, Apex Managed Sharing has been implemented to share records with the required users. Since the teams change frequently, managed sharing recalculations need to be manually run frequently. What can the Architect do to optimize this process?
optionA: Change the sharing model on the Job object to Public Read/Write.
optionB: Create a scheduled job to automatically run the sharing recalculations on a nightly basis.
optionC: Create public groups for each team, and share the jobs with the groups instead of users.
optionD: Create a custom Visualforce page to edit the jobs and specify Without Sharing on the controller.
explanation: Because the teams change frequently, sharing each job to a per-team public group means you only update group membership, which avoids recalculating record shares, instead of recalculating shares to individual users. Opening the OWD (A) removes security, a nightly recalculation job (B) still incurs the recalculation cost, and a without-sharing Visualforce page (D) bypasses security.

question: Universal Containers has a Private Sharing Model for the Opportunity object. Sales Rep A at Universal Containers created the Opportunity record and then transferred ownership of the Opportunity record to Sales Rep B. Both Sales Rep A and B have the same role in the Role Hierarchy. What access will Sales Rep A have to the Opportunity after the transfer?
optionA: Full Access
optionB: No Access
optionC: Read/Write
optionD: Read Only
explanation: After an opportunity is transferred to a same-role peer under a Private model, the former owner retains no access, because same-role peers do not see each other's records and the prior owner's access ends at the transfer. It is therefore not Full, Read/Write, or Read Only.

question: Universal Containers has just deployed a change to its role hierarchy. A manager is reporting that their staff can now see Accounts that they do not own and should not have access to. How should the Architect troubleshoot?
optionA: While logged in as the System Administrator, use the Field Accessibility Viewer to view a matrix of who has access to Accounts.
optionB: While logged in as the one of the Staff members, navigate to a sample account and use the Sharing button to determine who has access.
optionC: While logged in as the one of the Staff members, navigate to their user record and inspect their profile and role membership.
optionD: While logged in as the System Administrator, use the Field Accessibility Viewer to view a matrix of who has access to Accounts.
explanation: To troubleshoot why staff can suddenly see accounts they should not, log in as an affected staff member, open a sample account, and use the Sharing button to see exactly who has access and why through the row causes. The Field Accessibility viewer (A and D) is for fields, and inspecting the profile and role alone (C) will not reveal the sharing path.

question: After setting up Customer Community and enabling collaboration, the architect realizes that customers can only see Chatter posts from other customers in their account. What should the architect do to allow viewing Chatter posts from all customers?
optionA: Enable Community User Visibility.
optionB: Set View All for Chatter posts.
optionC: Enable Chatter Super User.
optionD: Enable Internal Users Visibility.
explanation: Customers seeing only Chatter posts from their own account is the default until Community (Site) User Visibility is enabled, which lets community users see other community users and their posts more broadly. A View All for Chatter, Chatter Super User, or internal-user visibility (B, C, and D) do not address external-to-external post visibility.

question: Universal Containers provides 24x7 support for its customers worldwide using call centers in three different countries with each one covering an 8-hour shift in different time zones. Service reps in a call center have access to the same information but only during their local working hours. Which feature in Salesforce controls access for specific login hours?
optionA: Set the login hours on user records
optionB: Permission sets
optionC: Custom permissions
optionD: Profiles
explanation: Login hours that restrict when users can access Salesforce are configured on the Profile, so each call center's profile limits access to its local shift. Login hours are not set directly on the user record (A) or through permission sets or custom permissions (B and C).
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Universal Containers (UC) has 200 distributors that use Partner Community licenses. Partners cannot see each other's data, but UC is also trying to give more visibility to data for certain individuals at a distributor. HOW can an Architect give users in the partner user role access to all Case and Container records owned by any user, regardless of role, at the same distributor?
optionA: Create an ownership-based sharing rule.
optionB: Create sharing sets.
optionC: Create a Permission Set granting "View All" permission to Case and Container records.
optionD: Give super user permission to the individual partner users.
explanation: Granting Super User permission to a partner user gives access to all Case and Container records owned by users at their role and below at the same distributor, which is the scalable per-individual approach. Ownership-based rules (A) or sharing sets (B) do not cleanly target all users at one distributor, and a View All permission set (C) over-grants across distributors. Note that the phrase "any user regardless of role" strains super-user semantics, which grant access only to the same role and below.

question: Universal Containers has created a custom Sales Operations profile with read and edit access to the Category field on a custom object. There is a new requirement that 3 of the 100 users assigned to the Sales Operations Profile should have read-only access to the Category field. How can the Architect support this request? Choose one answer:
optionA: Create a permission set in the Category field to read-only and assign it to the users.
optionB: Create a new profile without edit access to Category and assign it to the users.
optionC: Create a new page layout with the Category Field set to read-only for these users.
optionD: Create a custom permission to grant read-only access to Category and assign it to the users.
explanation: Field-Level Security through permission sets is additive, meaning it can only add access as a union and cannot remove the profile's existing edit access, so the only way to give three users read-only is a new profile without edit on the Category field. A read-only permission set (A, the source answer) cannot subtract edit, a read-only page layout (C) does not enforce FLS, and custom permissions (D) do not control FLS.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Sales manager at universal containers (UC) have requested viewing customer invoices in Salesforce. Invoice data is mastered in the ERP system. The architect at UC decided to surface the customer invoices in salesforce using external objects and did the following: -Configured an external object called Invoice. -Created a lookup relationship between account and the invoice. How can the architect grant the sales managers access to the customer invoices data?
optionA: By creating sharing rules to share the invoices records with users in sales mangers roles.
optionB: By creating manual sharing to share invoices with relevant sales managers.
optionC: By creating controlling the invoices object permission on the sales manager's profile.
optionD: By creating a sharing set to share invoices with users in sales manager role.
explanation: For an external object with a lookup to Account, access is granted through the object-level permission on the profile, because sharing rules, sharing sets, and manual sharing do not apply to external objects. That is why A, B, and D are not valid for granting external-object access.

question: (Variant) Sales Operations at Universal Containers (UC) wants to create list views to filter opportunities for certain geographies. In which two ways can UC hide list views that are not relevant to an individual user since there will be many list views? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Share the list views with the appropriate queue.
optionB: Share the list views with the appropriate individual users.
optionC: Share the list views with the appropriate public group.
optionD: Share the list views with the appropriate role in the role hierarchy
explanation: (Variant of Q44.) List views are hidden or shown by sharing them with a public group (C) or with a role in the role hierarchy (D), scoping each view to the relevant users. Queues (A) and individual-user sharing (B) are not the scalable list-view sharing targets here.

question: At Universal Containers, Accounts and Contacts are normally visible to all employees, and Proposals (custom object) are visible to the Account owner and managers. However, some Proposals are considered confidential and are managed by a Strategic Proposals team. These Proposals should not be visible to anyone in the Sales group other than the owner and the strategic team. How should the architect design for this requirement?
optionA: Proposal Owner set to the Strategic Deals Team Queue and set the Account relationship to Master-Detail.
optionB: Disable Grant Access Using Hierarchies and set an Owner-Based Sharing rule for Strategic Deals team.
optionC: Proposal Owner set to the Strategic Deals Team Queue and create an owner-based sharing rule to grant visibility to the Account owner
optionD: Disable Grant Access Using Hierarchies and set a Criteria-Based Sharing rule for Strategic Deals team.
explanation: Confidential proposals must be hidden from the wider Sales group, including managers above the owner, so disable "Grant Access Using Hierarchies" on Proposals to stop the hierarchy roll-up, then use an owner-based sharing rule to grant the Strategic Proposals team access. A criteria-based rule (D) would require a flag field and is less direct, and queue-owner approaches (A and C) do not isolate the records from the hierarchy. Note that disabling the hierarchy globally is in tension with normal proposals remaining visible to managers.

question: Universal Containers wants to create a way to store sensitive Invoice Data in Salesforce. A User who owns an Account should not see every Invoice, but only invoices that they or their subordinates own. Which two features should be considered during this solution implementation? Choose 2 answers.
optionA: Deploy the Relationship between Accounts and the Invoices Object as Master-Detail.
optionB: Ensure that the Organization-Wide Default sharing for Invoices is set to Private.
optionC: Create a Workflow that populates the Invoice sharing object upon Insert.
optionD: Deploy the Relationship between Accounts and the Invoices Object as Lookup.
explanation: Owner-or-subordinates visibility requires the Role Hierarchy on a Private object, so the Invoice OWD must be Private (B) and the Account-to-Invoice relationship must be a Lookup (D) so invoices have their own owner and sharing. A Master-Detail relationship (A) would force Controlled-by-Parent and could not be Private, contradicting the requirement, which is why the source pairing of A and B is internally inconsistent.

question: Universal Containers has the following Sharing Settings for their Org: Account = Private Contact = Controlled by Parent Opportunity = Private Case = Private They have enabled "Default Account Teams" and have trained users to set up their Default Team. Which three access levels can be set on the Account Team Member? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Opportunity Access
optionB: Case Access
optionC: Contact Access
optionD: Contract Access
explanation: Account Team member access can be set for the Account itself (E) and for child objects whose OWD is below Public Read/Write, here Opportunity (A) and Case (B), both Private. Contact is Controlled by Parent so it has no separate access setting, and there is no Contract Access option on account teams.

question: Partner users can access records belonging to users in their account at their same role or lower in the role hierarchy, for Cases, Leads, Opportunities and Custom Objects. Which of the following access has to be given?
explanation: The ability for partner users to access records, including Cases, Leads, Opportunities, and custom objects, owned by users at their same role or lower in their account's role hierarchy is granted by the partner/customer Super User permission. This is the standard mechanism for same-role-and-below partner visibility.

question: Universal Containers has recently implemented an integration that is populating 50 custom fields on the account object. They would like these fields to be available to Managers for reporting but do not want them to clutter the page layouts. What should the Architect recommend as a solution?
optionA: Add the fields to the Managers' Account Page Layout; mark the page layout section as Collapsed.
optionB: Grant the Managers access to the fields using a Role-Based Sharing Rule; leave them hidden on the page layout.
optionC: Add the fields to a Custom Report; grant the Managers' Role access to the report folder.
optionD: Grant Managers access to the fields using Field-Level Security; do not add them to a page layout.
explanation: Managers need the 50 fields for reporting but not on layouts, so grant access through Field-Level Security and simply leave the fields off the page layouts, because reporting reads FLS rather than layouts. Collapsed layout sections (A) still clutter, a sharing rule (B) does not control field visibility, and building a report (C) does not grant the underlying FLS.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: The Corporate Identity and Access Team needs to audit User setup in the Salesforce .. What two permissions should be granted to this team so they can perform their audit? Choose 2 answers
optionA: View permission on the User object
optionB: View Setup and Configuration
optionC: View All Users
optionD: View All Data
explanation: Auditing user setup requires "View Setup and Configuration" to inspect setup (B) and "View All Users" to see every user record (C). View on the User object (A) is narrower and not the audit permission, and View All Data (D) over-grants beyond the scope of a user-setup audit.
_cat: Permissions to Standard Objects, Custom Objects, and Fields

question: Assuming Person Account is enabled in a Salesforce organization, which three objects can be configured as "Controlled by Parent" in Sharing Settings? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Opportunity
optionB: Order
optionC: Asset
optionD: Contact
explanation: With Person Accounts enabled, the objects that can be set to Controlled by Parent relative to their parent account are Order (B), Asset (C), and Contact (D). Opportunity and Case have their own OWD and are not Controlled-by-Parent options.
